revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23986660476|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:هيتاتاره]][[fi:Hitatare]][[ja:直垂]][[ko:히타타레]]
[[File:Hitatare chakuyō.jpg|thumb|right|300px|『装束着用之図』（国立国会図書館蔵）より、「直垂」の図。]] [[File:Wakan Sansai Zue - Hitatare.png|thumb|200px|直垂 『{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|en|Wakan Sansai Zue|qid=Q1135803|lt=Wakan Sansai Zue|1=Wakan Sansai Zue}}』（{{ill|1702|en|1702|qid=Q6819|lt=1702|1=1702}}）]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 522.jpg|直垂を着用した男性。{{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}の{{ill|Jidai Matsuri|en|Jidai Matsuri|qid=Q912124|lt=Jidai Matsuri|1=Jidai Matsuri}}にて|200px|thumb]]
'''Hitatare''' (直垂) is a type of {{ill|Japanese clothing|ja|日本の装束|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese clothing|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} worn primarily by men in {{ill|samurai society|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai society|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Buke|qid=Q2927809}}. Over time, variations such as {{ill|Ōdamon Hitatare|ja|大紋直垂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdamon Hitatare|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Suō Hitatare|ja|素襖直垂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suō Hitatare|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yoroi Hitatare|ja|鎧直垂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoroi Hitatare|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直垂|1=Hitatare|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4118264}} developed alongside related garments like {{ill|kataginu|ja|肩衣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kataginu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 裃|1=kamishimo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q290445}}.
* Hitatare refers to both "clothing" and "bedding." * As bedding, "Hitatare Fusuma" emerged in the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, referring to {{ill|Futon|en|Futon|qid=Q837593|lt=futon|1=Futon}} or bedding items like the {{ill|quilt|ja|掛布団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quilt|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} preserved at {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} from the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}.
== Origin ==
The hitatare is believed to originate from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. Early hitatare developed from a front-fastened clothing style akin to modern {{ill|Kimono|en|Kimono|qid=Q483444|lt=kimono|1=Kimono}}. It predates garments like {{ill|Sokutai|en|Sokutai|qid=Q1156828|lt=sokutai|1=Sokutai}} and {{ill|nōshi|ja|直衣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nōshi|1=nōshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581837}}, evolving over centuries and eventually becoming a part of {{ill|samurai attire|ja|武士の装束|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai attire|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Initially, hitatare was commoner's attire, as depicted in {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} figurines and {{ill|picture scrolls|ja|絵巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/picture scrolls|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 絵巻物|1=Emakimono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1190781}} from the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} and {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. Over time, it adapted to samurai needs, eventually becoming formal wear.<ref>大百科事典（12）, 平凡社, 1985</ref>
== Characteristics ==
Despite variations over time, certain features remained consistent: * Two-piece construction with the upper garment tucked into {{ill|Hakama|en|Hakama|qid=Q164668|lt=hakama|1=Hakama}}. * The upper garment had {{ill|tairyō|ja|垂領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tairyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} collars and was fastened with cords. * Open sides on the upper garment. * Pants-like lower garments.
== History ==
=== Before the Heian Period ===
In the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, male {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} figurines depicted garments resembling hitatare. However, the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} saw the adoption of continental clothing styles under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}, relegating hitatare to commoners.
=== Heian to Kamakura Periods ===
By the {{ill|late Heian period|ja|平安時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/late Heian period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, hitatare became everyday wear for {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1975935}} and eventually formal attire. Sleeves widened for ceremonial use but were tied during combat to prevent interference. Specialized variants like {{ill|yoroi hitatare|ja|鎧直垂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoroi hitatare|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直垂|1=Hitatare|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4118264}} emerged for wearing under armor.
=== Muromachi Period ===
During the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, declining aristocratic power led to hitatare's use by court nobles as informal wear. Differences in fabric and adornment distinguished formal and informal variants, with samurai often restricted to specific styles.
=== Edo Period ===
Under the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, hitatare became exclusive to high-Ranking {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1975935}}. Strict rules governed its colors, usage, and associated ceremonies, reflecting social hierarchy.
=== Modern Era ===
Although its use declined, hitatare is still worn by performers of {{ill|traditional arts|ja|伝統芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/traditional arts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=Theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1364447}}, shrine attendants, and {{ill|Gyōji|en|Gyōji|qid=Q907647|lt=gyōji|1=Gyōji}} in {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|qid=Q40561|lt=sumo|1=Sumo}}. It has also gained popularity as wedding attire for grooms.
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōdamon|ja|大紋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdamon|1=daimon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11438472}} * {{ill|Suō|ja|素襖|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suō|1=suō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11605657}} * {{ill|Kataginu|ja|裃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kataginu|1=kamishimo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q290445}} * {{ill|Kiku Tōji|ja|菊綴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiku Tōji|1=kikutoji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619908}}
== External Links == * [http://www.iz2.or.jp/ Costume Museum]
